common_name: Stanley Plum (European Prune Plum, American Standard Freestone Plum)

planting.note: Plant bare-root while dormant in late winter or early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0) soil in full sun; Stanley is partially self-fruitful but produces heavier crops with a nearby European plum pollinator such as Italian Prune, Damson, or Greengage; it is the most widely planted European prune plum in the eastern United States and the commercial standard for fresh and dried prune production.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; prune annually to an open-center form; thin heavy fruit crops to 3–4 inches apart after natural fruit drop; Stanley ripens in late August to September in most of the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic; harvest for fresh eating when fully ripe and soft, or pick slightly firm for drying into prunes; self-fruitful but plant with a companion European plum for best yields. Stanley plum is the most important and widely planted European prune plum (Prunus domestica) in the eastern United States, developed at the New York State Agricultural Experiment Station at Geneva in 1926 and released in the 1930s. For decades, Stanley was synonymous with 'prune plum' throughout the eastern states — the word 'Stanley' and 'prune' were used interchangeably by many orchardists and home growers, reflecting its dominance as the only widely-grown European-type freestone plum in the region. The fruits are large to very large (for prune-type plums), oval to oblong, with deep blue-purple to nearly black skin and golden-yellow, firm, freestone flesh with a rich, sweet, slightly winey plum flavor. They are excellent for fresh eating at peak ripeness, cooking in preserves and tarts, and the definitive prune-type plum for drying — the firm, dry flesh and freestone characteristic make them ideal for dehydrating into prunes. Stanley requires approximately 800 chill hours and is reliably productive in zones 4–7, with reliable but sometimes marginal performance in zone 8 in mild-winter years. It is partially self-fruitful, producing some crop when grown alone but yielding significantly more when cross-pollinated with other European plum varieties. The trees are moderately vigorous and productive, with moderate disease susceptibility under humid conditions.
sources: New York State Agricultural Experiment Station — Stanley release notes
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Tree Fruit Production Guide

common_name: Italian Prune Plum (Fellenberg Plum, Pacific Northwest Prune Plum, Dried Prune Standard)

planting.note: Plant bare-root while dormant in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0) soil in full sun; Italian Prune is largely self-fruitful and will produce without a cross-pollinator, though yields improve with a companion European plum nearby; it requires approximately 700–800 chill hours and is best adapted to zones 5–8 with reliable winter cold.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil in full sun; Italian Prune is self-fruitful but benefits from cross-pollination; the oval blue-black fruits ripen in September in the Pacific Northwest; allow fruits to fully soften and develop their sweet flavor before fresh eating; for prune production, pick slightly underripe and dry at low heat (135–145°F) for 18–24 hours in a dehydrator. Italian Prune plum (also known as Fellenberg after a German horticulturist, and as 'Prune' or 'Italian' in the Pacific Northwest) is the foundational prune variety of the Pacific Northwest commercial prune and dried fruit industry — the variety responsible for making the Willamette Valley of Oregon the center of American prune production and the source of most of the dried prunes consumed in the United States. The tree was brought to the Pacific Northwest in the mid-1800s by European immigrants (primarily German and Italian settlers) for whom dried prunes were a staple preserved food, and the variety proved ideally suited to the mild, moderately humid climate of the Willamette Valley where warm, dry summers during ripening allowed on-tree drying and hand-harvest at low cost. The fruits are medium-sized, oblong, classic 'prune-shaped' with deep blue-purple to nearly black skin and amber-yellow to golden flesh that is firm, moderately dry (essential for making quality prunes), and freestone at full ripeness — all characteristics essential for the drying process. At fresh-eating maturity, Italian Prune is sweet, richly flavored, and excellent for preserves, tarts, and plum butter as well as fresh consumption. A traditional home garden plum throughout the Pacific Coast states and the Northeast.
sources: Oregon State University Extension — Prune/Plum Production in Oregon

common_name: Mount Royal Plum (Canadian Cold-Hardy European Freestone Prune Plum)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mount Royal is self-fruitful (one of the most reliably self-fruitful European plums available) and is especially valued in zones 4–5 where it can be grown reliably without a cross-pollinator in climates too cold for many other European plums; it originated in Montreal, Quebec, where it was selected for hardiness in the severe Canadian climate.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mount Royal is self-fruitful and very cold-hardy; the round to oval blue-purple fruits ripen in late August to mid-September; flavor is rich, sweet, and high-quality — one of the best-tasting cold-hardy European plums; suitable for fresh eating, preserves, cooking, and drying. Mount Royal plum is a Canadian-origin European prune plum (Prunus domestica) that has been widely grown in Quebec and the northeastern states since its selection in the Montreal area (likely in the late 19th century), where it consistently demonstrated the cold-hardiness and reliable self-fruitfulness that make it particularly valuable in zones 4–5 where standard European plums like Stanley and Italian Prune may suffer winter damage or require cross-pollination to set adequate crops. The name reflects its Canadian origin — Mount Royal (Mont-Royal) is the mountain in the heart of Montreal, and the variety's proven performance in that zone 5 climate is its primary recommendation. Mount Royal produces medium-sized, round to slightly oval, blue-purple to dark blue fruits with firm, yellow-green, freestone flesh and a sweet, rich European plum flavor with balanced acid and sugar that makes it excellent for all purposes: fresh eating at peak ripeness, preserves and jam, plum butter, cooking in tarts and cakes, and drying into prunes. It is considered one of the best-tasting cold-hardy European plums available, with quality comparable to Stanley but with significantly better cold-hardiness and self-fruitfulness. An essential variety for home orchards in zones 4–5 where plum options are limited.
sources: University of Vermont Extension — Stone Fruit Production
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Tree Fruit Production Guide

common_name: Burbank Plum (Luther Burbank's Japanese Plum, Red Plum Classic)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Burbank requires cross-pollination from another Japanese plum such as Santa Rosa, Beauty, or Methley for reliable fruit set — it is not reliably self-fruitful; requires approximately 600–700 chill hours, making it adaptable to zones 5–9 with adequate winter cold; in zone 9a, a nearby companion is essential.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; thin fruit in spring to 4–6 inches apart; plant with a companion Japanese plum for reliable fruiting; Burbank produces medium to large round, dark red to mahogany-red fruits in mid-July to early August with amber-yellow flesh; the flavor is very sweet with tropical-fruity notes and low acidity — one of the best fresh-eating Japanese plums. Burbank plum is Luther Burbank's most famous and historically significant Japanese plum variety, released by the legendary plant breeder in the 1880s from his Santa Rosa, California plant breeding operations where he created an extraordinary number of new fruit varieties through deliberate hybridization and selection from germplasm imported from Japan. Burbank introduced Japanese plums (Prunus salicina) to American horticulture, obtaining seeds directly from Japan through the USDA and developing multiple varieties — but 'Burbank' became the most widely planted and commercially important of these, establishing the Japanese plum as a standard American backyard fruit and farm market crop throughout zones 5–9. The fruits are medium to large, round, with a distinctive deep red to mahogany skin and amber-yellow flesh that is firm, juicy, and very sweet with characteristic Japanese plum flavor — a somewhat tropical, stone-fruit sweetness with lower acidity than European plums. The combination of low chill requirement (approximately 600–700 hours), broad adaptability from zone 5 to zone 9, and excellent fresh-eating quality makes Burbank one of the most successful home garden stone fruits of the 20th century. Santa Rosa plum (also by Luther Burbank) is considered the superior variety by many modern tasters, but Burbank predates it and remains widely planted and recommended.
sources: Luther Burbank Society — Historical Plant Records

common_name: Poinsettia (Mexican Christmas Star, Easter Flower, Noche Buena Garden Shrub)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained soil; in zone 9a and the Gulf South, poinsettia is a perennial GARDEN SHRUB growing 6–12 feet tall that blooms every winter in response to the shortening days of November–December — it is NOT just a houseplant; established plants naturalize and self-sow freely in zone 9–10 and bloom reliably each winter without indoor protection.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun or morning sun with afternoon shade; prune heavily after blooming (January–February in zone 9) — cut plants back by one-third to one-half to prevent legginess and encourage compact growth and more prolific flowering the following winter; avoid any artificial light at night in October–November which will delay or prevent flowering since poinsettia requires uninterrupted long nights (14+ hours of darkness) to initiate flower (bract) color. Poinsettia (Euphorbia pulcherrima) is primarily known as a holiday houseplant in most of the United States, but in zone 9–11 (tropical and subtropical Gulf Coast, California, Hawaii, Florida) it is a perennial garden SHRUB that grows 6–12 feet tall, often becoming a significant landscape element that blooms dependably every winter. In the wild, poinsettia is native to Mexico — particularly the Pacific coastal region of Taxco, Guerrero — where it grows as a semi-deciduous shrub or small tree 10–15 feet tall in the dry tropical forest and blooms in response to the shortening days of November–December, turning entire hillsides red with its bracts (the colorful parts are modified leaves, not petals — the true flowers are the small yellow structures at the center). The plant takes its common English name from Joel Roberts Poinsett, the first U.S. Ambassador to Mexico, who introduced it to the United States in 1825. In zone 9a, poinsettia grown outdoors blooms reliably from November through January with minimal care, and established plants develop woody trunks and can become large impressive shrubs. The characteristic that determines bloom timing is photoperiodism — poinsettia requires uninterrupted dark periods of 14 hours or more to initiate bract coloration, which occurs naturally as nights lengthen in autumn. Streetlights or interior lights shining on the plant at night will disrupt this cycle and delay or prevent coloring.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Poinsettia Production
California Rare Fruit Growers — Subtropical Ornamentals
